era_context: The War of Spanish Succession (1701–1714) began in 1701 and was in its second year in 1702. This was a major European conflict over the succession to the Spanish throne following the death of Charles II of Spain. The Qing Dynasty under Kangxi was at its height; the Ottoman Empire remained a significant regional power; and European colonial powers were consolidating control in the Americas and Asia. The Great Northern War (1700–1721) between Russia and Sweden was ongoing.

events.title: War of Spanish Succession: Opening Campaigns
events.description: In 1702, the War of Spanish Succession entered its second year with major military campaigns across multiple European theaters. The conflict pitted the Grand Alliance (England, Dutch Republic, Holy Roman Empire, and others) against France and Spain (under the Bourbon claimant Philip V). Major campaigns occurred in the Spanish Netherlands, Italy, and Germany. The war was fought over the succession to the Spanish throne following the death of Charles II in 1700, with the Grand Alliance supporting the Archduke Charles of Austria against the Bourbon claimant Philip V.

sources.name: Contemporary military dispatches and diplomatic correspondence
Military histories and historiographic consensus on the War of Spanish Succession

events.certainty_note: The War of Spanish Succession is extensively documented in contemporary military records, diplomatic correspondence, and later military histories. The opening campaigns of 1702 are well-attested.

events.title: Death of William III of England
events.description: William III, King of England, Scotland, and Ireland, died on 8 March 1702 after a reign of 13 years. He was succeeded by his sister-in-law Anne Stuart, who became Queen Anne. William's death marked a significant transition in English politics and the War of Spanish Succession. Anne's reign (1702–1714) would be marked by the dominance of the Duke of Marlborough and the continuation of the war against France.

sources.name: English court records and contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: William III's death date is precisely documented in English records. The succession of Anne is well-established in historical scholarship.

events.title: Queen Anne's War Begins
events.description: Queen Anne's War (1702–1713), the North American theater of the War of Spanish Succession, began in 1702 with raids and military operations along the frontier between British and French colonial territories. The war involved English colonists, French colonists, and numerous Native American tribes allied with one side or the other. The conflict was characterized by frontier raids, naval operations, and competition for control of colonial territories.
events.certainty_note: Queen Anne's War is well-documented in colonial records. The beginning of the war in 1702 is established in historical scholarship.
cross_references: 1713-002 (Treaty of Utrecht, which ended Queen Anne's War)

events.title: Battle of Vigo Bay
events.description: On 23 October 1702, an English and Dutch naval fleet under Admiral Sir George Rooke attacked a French and Spanish fleet in Vigo Bay, Galicia. The engagement resulted in the destruction or capture of several French and Spanish ships and the seizure of treasure from the Spanish colonies. The battle demonstrated Allied naval superiority and disrupted French-Spanish coordination in the War of Spanish Succession.

sources.name: Military histories of the War of Spanish Succession

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Vigo Bay is well-documented in naval records from multiple nations. Date and outcome are uncontested.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's Fourth Southern Inspection Tour
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor undertook his fourth southern inspection tour in 1702, traveling through the Yangtze River region to assess administrative conditions and reinforce imperial authority. These tours were major state events combining inspection, ritual, and propaganda. The 1702 tour reinforced Qing control over the prosperous southern provinces.

sources.name: Kangxi's official records and court diaries (Qing archives)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Qing imperial records. The southern tours are well-attested historical events with precise dating.

events.title: Asiento Negotiations Begin
events.description: In 1702, negotiations began regarding the Asiento de Negros (contract for the slave trade), a valuable monopoly that would become a major issue in the War of Spanish Succession. The Asiento granted the holder the right to supply enslaved Africans to Spanish American colonies. Control of the Asiento was a significant economic and political prize, and negotiations over it would continue throughout the war.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on colonial trade and the slave trade

events.certainty_note: The Asiento became a major issue in the War of Spanish Succession; however, the exact timing of when negotiations began in 1702 is less precisely documented than later developments.
cross_references: 1713-002 (Treaty of Utrecht, which awarded the Asiento to Britain)

disconfirming_evidence: The War of Spanish Succession is sometimes presented as a clear conflict between French expansion and European balance of power; however, the war's origins were complex, involving legitimate dynastic claims on both sides and significant internal divisions within the Grand Alliance. Queen Anne's War is sometimes portrayed as a simple extension of European conflicts to North America; however, indigenous motivations and the complex alliances between European colonists and Native American tribes were more nuanced than simple European direction.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1702 is rich in European military and political history, particularly regarding the War of Spanish Succession, which is extensively covered in British, French, Dutch, Spanish, and Austrian archives. English political history is well-documented through court records and contemporary chronicles. Colonial North American history is documented through colonial records. Qing China is documented through imperial records. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; indigenous perspectives in North America are largely filtered through colonial sources. African history, particularly regarding the slave trade, is underrepresented in surviving documentation from this period.

graph_edges.note: The opening campaigns of the War of Spanish Succession led to the Battle of Blenheim in 1704
Queen Anne's War began shortly after Anne's accession to the throne
Queen Anne's War was concluded by the Treaty of Utrecht in 1713
The Battle of Vigo Bay was part of the broader War of Spanish Succession
Asiento negotiations were resolved in the Treaty of Utrecht (1713)
